author: @beforebefore
text:
0.22uF is not 220pf, it's 220nF   µF=10e-6;  nF=10e-9;   pF=10e-12   From an engineering perspective, the diodes need to have the smallest bandgap as possible... Germanium diodes held this trophy for decades with 0.25V vF (find some old 1N34 diodes for this).  Also note that your circuit is missing the Ground/Earth connection, which should be the middle connection point of the 2 large capacitors.


More recently, Schottky diodes have become much improved, and are useful for some RF rectification with extremely low forward voltage (Vf)... as low as 0.100V or even less (at low currents, which is what you are working with).  Compared to your silicon diodes which require 0.65V forward voltage before they conduct, that's an improvement of 6.5 times.
PMEG1201AESF  This part has Vf as low as 0.05V at 1mA

The problem with this circuit is that it only allows for 4x the diode Vf as output voltage since they are all connected in series across the 2 large capacitors.  This means the maximum voltage across the 2 large capacitors will; be at most 2.8V (with silicon diodes), then it will be short-circuited through the 4 diodes.


I have not tried this form of radiant energy harvesting with a Bridge Rectifier configuration, but it would allow unlimited Voltage... within the limits of the diodes reverse voltage rating.  What would normally be the "AC inputs" of the bridge rectifier would be one of the 0.2uF capacitors to the Antenna, and another 0.2uF to Earth ground.

author: @vylbird8014
text: I've experimented with something similiar myself, RF harvesting. Something to look out for though: At one point I thought I was collecting a very considerable amount of power, and amazed at how well it worked - until I turned off the CFL light. My harvesting equipment was working so well because it was coupling to the weak but very nearby power transmitted from the cheap switching power supply. You could be picking up power coupled from the cabling conduit your antenna is positioned above.

author: @jtpinion4294
text:
Hi there, I hope you and family are all well. This reminds me of a little kit I used to sell when I worked for Maplin electronics before they went bust. It was basically a radiant energy collector which powered a digital clock. It had practically the same circuitry as what you have created except it came with a rather long wire as the antenna. I may still have it somewhere. I just thought I'd mention as it triggered some nostalgia of when I used to work there and helping people create their circuits for the projects as I was the 'component guy' who's very much enjoy talking to people such as yourself about all kinds of circuits and projects. Thank you for continuing to share your findings and projects. 
John.

4:40 Depending on the frequency you' are tuned to, you may want to consider using the Tantalum capacitors instead of an electrolytic capacitor, as the Electrolytics are better at lower frequencies, and the tanatalums (orange package) are better at higher freqencies.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
One day I want to make a giant sputtered [or with graphene maybe?] fractal antenna on very large sheets of plastic [like the kind you used or with Mylar or others] with multi-tuned capacitance [either fixed multi-capacitance, or with real time dynamic digital tuning, not sure yet] so as to make a massively EM sensitive antenna which can be hung from the ceiling of a building, or possibly even rolled up and placed at a higher-than-ground-level point where it can still directly interact with passing EM radiation in the environment [whether naturally or humanly caused matters not to me for the sake of generating electricity, but I do tend to favor natural as much as possible, if only because it is more likely to exist if known human generated EM for any reason becomes unavailable (such as a power black-out, for instance)].

The only drawback to having such a large and fragile antenna is that it can not be left open to the elements for long [the collective effects of weathering, which includes V breakdown, hail, wind, rain, animal droppings, adverse effects of pollutants, pollen,  etc] and therefore must be held in some manner which adequately protects it from the myriad host of adverse agents in nature as well as those which are man-made.

The biggest drawback to rolling it up into a tube, however [as many who read this will immediately see without being told] is that, much like a solar panel which needs maximum surface interaction, most EM radiation will fly past a rolled up coil around all of the sides, which drastically limits its effectiveness if left in a concealed state.

IDEALLY would be to find a way to fasten it to the sides of very tall, large buildings, where many of the adverse effects of nature would be mitigated, [wind would be mitigated by  it being bonded to the wall's surface, rain by a second sheet if thin plastic over it, as would acid, animal droppings, etc] and where it would sufficiently high up enough to take advantage of such effects as thinning atmosphere where more EM radiation is passed through and not absorbed by the atmosphere and pollution within the atmosphere [note how these type collectors harvest markedly higher power at high noon on a hot summer day when the air is typically most heated, ionic activity [such as lightning] is highest, and charges remain in a more energetic state higher in the stratosphere, where the bandwidths of energy being released are both denser and at higher amplitudes [based on the energy density of an EM signal based on both frequency and amplitude expressed as root mean squared] so that if one could hang these giant fractal EM collecting antennas on all of the skyscrapers in a major city [ Los Angeles, New York and Mexico City all come to mind, but any tall buildings will work, especially at more southerly latitudes or higher altitudes like Denver, CO] one might see a very real and appreciable volume of power to be harvested!

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: For sure, especially if you know the direction to your nearest constantly broadcasting radio tower and the frequencies it uses. Then you could use masking tape to mark the spaces between tuned dipoles, paint over with graphene doped paint, and remove the masking tape to have a whole wall of dipoles. Then you could lay fairly thin copper tape down them as pickups, and paint over it with a defect hiding paint, or smooth the surface with putty and a screed and then paint. Rectifying the output of each dipole and putting them in parralel would probably give you enough power for some nice red LED emergency lighting.

Storing this "trickle" charge is the KEY.  Continuous charging of caps or battery banks, from this method, can be accomplished.  No wind...No Sun...No big deal ;) I'll call it, "The G.R.A.I.L."  Graphene Reactive Aerial Inductive Laminates   ;)

author: @Xeno_Bardock
text: Check Eric Dollard on Tesla's longitudinal electricity. He shows a LMD circuit which allows you to convert AC into LC Longitudinal Current. Google "Longitudinal Waves and Transverse Waves tests by Jean-Louis Naudin" for LMD circuit diagram and "Transverse & Longitudinal Electric Waves and Tesla’s Longitudinal Electricity" for Eric Dollard videos. Also check video "Replication of Eric Dollard's Analog Computer". You can measure the differences between TEM and LMD circuit with Tri-Field meter.

replies.text: lol - cheers mate

author: @climatebabes
text: Should this work better with an array of antenna's of different length, each adding to the output? Tesla had a design where the sun would shine on a copper plate, causing electrons to be dislodged and the plate to go positive. An early solar panel of sorts. Not the same as this but also cool to try maybe?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh - that's a nice one - I'll give it a go - I have just ordered some 1n34as - I'll wait for them to arrive and try it - cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I did a quick test - a video tomorrow - but a plate 15cm by 15cm was enough to light the led and keep it alight - no voltage drop as the led was lit - very interesting - cheers

author: @jonbigman9723
text:
Good thing you mentioned energy scavenging because I suspect it is getting all of it's energy from the AC conduit running between the antenna and rectification circuit.
You could prove me wrong by setting it up in an area that has no AC conduits running  by the antenna.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: or you could

author: @brainretardant
text: Have you ever experimented with tuned fractal antennas and scavenging?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no mate - but nice idea

replies.author: @brainretardant
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering instead of your graphene antenna try a russet potato.  I watched this guy once show a potato worked better as a replacement radio antenna than a straightened out coat hanger.  Not sure if it was true but a potato has a conductive sugar crystalline organization like graphene, I'm not sure if the starches are planar?  Crazy but there might be RF harvesting going on in that tuber as well as visible light

author: @benthere8051
text: 1N34A germanium diodes are rated at 50 mA and only have a 0.2V Vf rating. That would be a better choice for your setup than the Schottky diodes. You are getting enough voltage from your antenna to power a small radio, which I find amazing.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate - thanks for the info I'll grab a few - this looks promising for sure

replies.author: @thebeststooge
replies.text: I read that 1N34A is old stock as no one makes them these days.  Germanium diodes had a lot of potential.

replies.author: @benthere8051
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering - Your 1N4007 diodes have a Vf of almost 0.7 volts. The Ge diodes would give you about a full volt better performance for a full-wave bridge.

replies.author: @benthere8051
replies.text: @thebeststooge 1N34A diodes are still available in bulk, even from Amazon. I just bought 50 for less than $10.00.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @benthere8051 I know mate - but thanks for suggesting it
